speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: ’Tis one of the best discretions of a ’oman as

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: ever I did look upon.

speaker: PAGE
text: And did he send you both these letters at an

speaker: PAGE
text: instant?

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Within a quarter of an hour.

speaker: FORD
text: Pardon me, wife. Henceforth do what thou wilt.

speaker: FORD
text: I rather will suspect the sun with cold

speaker: FORD
text: Than thee with wantonness. Now doth thy honor stand,

speaker: FORD
text: In him that was of late an heretic,

speaker: FORD
text: As firm as faith.

speaker: PAGE
text: ’Tis well, ’tis well. No more.

speaker: PAGE
text: Be not as extreme in submission as in offense.

speaker: PAGE
text: But let our plot go forward. Let our wives

speaker: PAGE
text: Yet once again, to make us public sport,

speaker: PAGE
text: Appoint a meeting with this old fat fellow,

speaker: PAGE
text: Where we may take him and disgrace him for it.

speaker: FORD
text: There is no better way than that they spoke of.

speaker: PAGE
text: How, to send him word they’ll meet him in the

speaker: PAGE
text: park at midnight? Fie, fie, he’ll never come.

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: You say he has been thrown in the rivers

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: and has been grievously peaten as an old ’oman.

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: Methinks there should be terrors in him, that he

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: should not come. Methinks his flesh is punished;

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: he shall have no desires.

speaker: PAGE
text: So think I too.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              FORD
text: Devise but how you’ll use him when he comes,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              FORD
text: And let us two devise to bring him thither.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: There is an old tale goes that Herne the Hunter,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Sometime a keeper here in Windsor Forest,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Doth all the wintertime, at still midnight,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Walk round about an oak, with great ragged horns,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And there he blasts the tree, and takes the cattle,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And makes milch-kine yield blood, and shakes a chain

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: In a most hideous and dreadful manner.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: You have heard of such a spirit, and well you know

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: The superstitious idle-headed eld

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Received and did deliver to our age

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: This tale of Herne the Hunter for a truth.

speaker: PAGE
text: Why, yet there want not many that do fear

speaker: PAGE
text: In deep of night to walk by this Herne’s oak.

speaker: PAGE
text: But what of this?

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              FORD
text: Marry, this is our device,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              FORD
text: That Falstaff at that oak shall meet with us.

speaker: PAGE
text: Well, let it not be doubted but he’ll come.

speaker: PAGE
text: And in this shape when you have brought him thither,

speaker: PAGE
text: What shall be done with him? What is your plot?

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: That likewise have we thought upon, and thus:

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Nan Page my daughter, and my little son,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And three or four more of their growth we’ll dress

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Like urchins, aufs, and fairies, green and white,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: With rounds of waxen tapers on their heads

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And rattles in their hands. Upon a sudden,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: As Falstaff, she, and I are newly met,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Let them from forth a sawpit rush at once

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: With some diffusèd song. Upon their sight,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: We two in great amazedness will fly.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Then let them all encircle him about,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And, fairy-like, to pinch the unclean knight,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And ask him why, that hour of fairy revel,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: In their so sacred paths he dares to tread

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: In shape profane.

speaker: FORD
text: And till he tell the truth,

speaker: FORD
text: Let the supposèd fairies pinch him sound

speaker: FORD
text: And burn him with their tapers.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: The truth being known,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: We’ll all present ourselves, dis-horn the spirit,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And mock him home to Windsor.

speaker: FORD
text: The children must

speaker: FORD
text: Be practiced well to this, or they’ll ne’er do ’t.

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: I will teach the children their behaviors, and

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: I will be like a jackanapes also, to burn the knight

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: with my taber.

speaker: FORD
text: That will be excellent. I’ll go buy them vizards.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: My Nan shall be the queen of all the fairies,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Finely attirèd in a robe of white.

speaker: PAGE
text:
That silk will I go buy.
                 
                (
                Aside
                .
                )
               And in that time

speaker: PAGE
text: Shall Master Slender steal my Nan away

speaker: PAGE
text: And marry her at Eton.—Go, send to Falstaff straight.

speaker: FORD
text: Nay, I’ll to him again in name of Brook.

speaker: FORD
text: He’ll tell me all his purpose. Sure he’ll come.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Fear not you that. Go get us properties

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And tricking for our fairies.

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: Let us about it. It is admirable pleasures and

speaker:
SIR
               
              HUGH
text: fery honest knaveries.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Go, Mistress Ford,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Send quickly to Sir John to know his mind.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: I’ll to the doctor. He hath my good will,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And none but he, to marry with Nan Page.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: That Slender, though well-landed, is an idiot,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: And he my husband best of all affects.

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: The doctor is well-moneyed, and his friends

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Potent at court. He, none but he, shall have her,

speaker:
MISTRESS
               
              PAGE
text: Though twenty thousand worthier come to crave her.
